speaker: interviewer
text: When I go like this, it means look at me. When I go like this, it means we're toward the end of a tape, which means we're not going to start a new chapter. We'll just wind up, and then we'll start the new chapter on the other tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: And how will you cue me, sir? On the shoulder, what? February the 15, 1995. The survivor is Leizer Hoffer. Interviewer, Felicia Carmelly. The interview takes place in Toronto, Canada. And it is in English.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Leizer Hoffer. I was born in Vishnitz, November 14, 1927. My father's name was David Hoffer. My mother's name was Sally [INAUDIBLE]. Her maiden name was Drassinover. My grandfather my-- on my mother's side, his name was Leib Drassinover. And his wife was Shaina Dvoive Drassinover. On my father's side, my grandfather's name was Yehosia Hoffer. And his name-- his wife was Vicky Hoffer. Both my grandparents lived on the outskirts of a very well-known Jewish shtetl called Vishnitz. So my early years were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that place have a name? That shtetl where your grandparents live, what was the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]. It was just a-- a couple of kilometers on the outskirts of Vishnitz. The early years of my life, I remember living in my grandfather's house and my mother's father's house and also visiting my other grandparents, which was kind of an ongoing thing from week to week where all the family met and spent days together, especially Saturday afternoons. I very well remember those days where family gathered and enjoyed their visits and company and spent time together. Those were very important days. That was-- that was a time in our family which now I realize, after the tragedy, how everything got disrupted and destroyed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do on Saturdays when you got together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything and-- and-- and anything. They played cards. They talked politics. They enjoyed discussions. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We just had-- the kids played. And-- and the grandparents, of course, enjoyed the grandchildren just like a normal family. But there was a lot of-- a lot of love. There was a lot of togetherness. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] In the-- in the first few years of my life, if I go back as far as I can remember, I remember attending the local school, which was just about a-- a kilometer from where my grandfather's house was. That was just the-- the first year, grade one, grade two.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that a public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was a public school. But I think there were only six grades, if I'm not mistaken. It's a long time ago. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Having had to walk to school every day and back from school, and I was just a little boy-- six, seven years old-- I will never forget the fear that I had in walking that short distance. Because we were only two or three Jewish families surrounded by mostly Ruthenian, Ukrainian people which did not appreciate us in their midst. To me, they not only called me names, but they also threw rocks at me and at many times threatened me with their dogs. So the walk to school and back was a very painful experience. Which, at that time, I couldn't understand why. But now that I look back, I realize that it was the first demonstration of, if you may call it, anti-Semitism, or hatred towards Jews. My grandfather [INAUDIBLE] Leib, my mother's father, was a great [INAUDIBLE] of the Vishnitz Rabbi. And he disregarded the surroundings he lived in and dressed himself in the traditional Hasidic garb. And-- [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK. Would you like a drink?

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS] --and walked every Saturday [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to Vishnitz to attend Shabbos prayers. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And, on many occasions, I went along with him, just a little boy. And those are memories which will always be with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So both my grandparents left me with very fond memories. On the one side, the traditional, orthodox life and thinking and being. And my other grandfather, which wasn't a great orthodox-- it wasn't extremely religious, but the-- a more modern type of believing and being a Jew in a more modern way. Those memories are very, very important for me. And I will never forget them.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I got a little older, I started going to cheder and school. And that required walking an additional 2 kilometers, which was now 3 kilometers into Vishnitz and back. And so I attended school in the morning, until about 1, 2 o'clock. And in the afternoon, I used to go to cheder and walk home again. At that time in my life, I got more acquainted with-- I got a little older and more acquainted with Vishnitz and Jewish life in Vishnitz. Vishnitz was a great Jewish stetl. It was a shtut, but, in terms of Europe, it was a shtetl. It was very famous for the Vishnitzer dynasty, the rabbinic dynasty, who occupied a very prominent place in Vishnitz. And not only Vishnitz, in Romania, as well, Northern Bukovina.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the Vishnitzer Rabbi name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rabbi [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Vishnitz was a dynamic Yiddish shtetl. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It reflected everything that Yiddish life had to offer in that era. We had, of course, the very religious-- the Hasidim, and the very orthodox, the more modern Jewish people. We had, of course, those that [INAUDIBLE], those that didn't-- were non-believers. Not many, but there were some. We had people that embraced Zionism. We had people that were communists. We had people that sought the salvation or a bettering for-- for their life in many different philosophies. But every group was active, dynamic. And you could feel Jewish life in Vishnitz. Those that were religious were attending-- attending the synagogues and governing. There were many little synagogues that were always full. People believed. And people were Jews to the full extent.

speaker: interviewer
text: About what was the Jewish population in Vishnitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I don't remember for sure, but I would guess that the population was anywhere between 5,000 and 10,000 people. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: The Jewish population?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish population, yes. It was-- it was a well-known Yiddish-- Yiddish shtetl. Life went on like that. And this goes approximately into the '35, '36. But in '35, '36, '37, things started to change. The normal pattern of trying to make a living and working and-- and caring for-- for families and for children started in some way to be influenced by new winds, so to speak. I'm referring, of course, to the political situation that started to develop in Germany, as well as the political situation that started to develop in the Soviet Union. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] When the first news started to come out that the Jewish people were being discriminated in Germany, that the Nazis were starting to prosecute Jews simply because they were Jews-- pardon me-- the first opinion at home was that it almost can't be possible. I particularly remember a little later, a couple of years later, the famous Kristallnacht, when my parents and our neighbors were sitting around a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] radio-- a Telefunken radio. And they were listening to the news coming out about the atrocities of the Kristallnacht. I was just a little boy, but I do remember their expressions. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And what they said was, it can't be possible. The German people are not those kind of people. My parents and our neighbors were from the Austrian era. And they remembered the Germans in that time to be civilized and good people. They lived. They respected-- where they lived in Austria, they were respected. Their religion was respected. Their rights were respected. They served the Kaiser. They served the Austrian army. My dad was a soldier in the First World War-- fought on the Italian front, used to talk about it sometime.

speaker: interviewer
text: So their opinion was, it's not possible. Something isn't right. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The reason I am mentioning that is because that was an underlying-- underlying [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] part of our tragedy. Explain that, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: The explanation is that nobody until the last moment believed that what was happening was really happening. And the Germans and the Nazis and all our enemies used that. They used that to further their ultimate goal, which was our destruction. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] While this was happening and events started to unfold themselves a little bit better, so that people started to question whether maybe they were wrong in thinking that what was happening in Germany was not happening. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A lot of young Jewish people and even maybe some older Jewish people started to think that maybe they should hope for communism to solve or rescue us from-- from whatever the Nazis might bring. In 1939, the Soviet Union and Germany divided Poland. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Living in Vishnitz, which was on the Polish border on the other side of the river-- the river was the Cheremosh. On the other side of the River Cheremosh was a little shtetl called Kitev-- Kuty. Kuty also had a Jewish population. I know, because my grandfather's sister lived in Kuty. And we were once over on the other side to visit her. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In '39, when Poland was divided, instead of Poland being our neighbor or our border across the river, it became the Soviet Union. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Those that were communists and those that believed in the communist ideology-- and we had them, some, in our own family-- almost felt that salvation is at hand. So the Jewish community started to be divided-- those that had sympathies for the communist side, those that had sympathies for the orthodox side. And people were somewhat confused. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But it wasn't very long until things really started to change and we began to see just how dangerous our situation was. I remember in particular when a segment of our Ukrainian neighbors, or the people that lived amongst we lived, started believing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they started to organize themselves to support the side of the fascists in hope of attaining an independent Ukrainian state for themselves. But, of course, by embracing that, they declared, again, the Jewish people as their enemies. Because all the fascists declared the Jewish people as their enemies. I remember a day when a public announcer went around and shouted out that on a certain date for 24 hours there is to be no Jewish man, woman, or child on the street. Because their safety is not going to be protected.

speaker: interviewer
text: On a certain day, did you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: On a certain date.

speaker: interviewer
text: Date. From a certain date?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, at a certain date. They called it-- the Ukrainian people called a congress. On a certain day-- I don't remember what the date was. But on that day for 24 hours, there is to be no Jewish man, woman, or child in the streets. They have to be in their homes, because they don't guarantee their safety. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And so the day came, and the big congress was on. And the big celebration was on, of course, for the Ukrainians that embraced the fascist ideology. And who was the scapegoat?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish people were the scapegoat. At the end of the congress towards evening, the hooligans let loose in the city, smashed windows, looted houses, beat up people. And that was the first taste for me of what we were going to face if fascism ever got hold.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you describe that as a regular pogrom?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a mini pogrom. I don't think there were any people killed. But there were a lot of-- in our particular house, I remember we had a house that was facing the-- the front, the main street and the side street. The rocks that were thrown into our house ended up in the kitchen, which came from both sides. So when this was over, there was glass all over. We had a Kristallnacht in our own house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe it was in '39. It was either '38 or '39. It was just a year or two before-- before the-- the war really started.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember about what time of the year that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in the summertime-- late summer. It was late summer. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Shortly after that, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on a given day, I remember the Soviet Union gave an ultimatum to Romania to-- that Romania should evacuate or leave the two provinces-- the two northern provinces bordering on the Soviet Union-- the Bukovina and Bessarabia. That was in 1940. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We didn't know whether Romania was going to go to war or whether they were going to give up peacefully. But Romania--

speaker: interviewer
text: They were supposed to give it up to the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the Russians. The Russians gave them 24 hours or 36 hours to reply. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were very naive. Because I remember in circumstances like that, had we had more experience, we would have been-- we would have been in hiding. Or, we would have trying to seek shelter. But we took it for granted, and we didn't go anywhere. And we didn't seek any shelter. But anyway, the Romanians gave up the territories. They-- they were no match for the Soviet giant. And so in about two days or a day and a half, the Romanians evacuated. And the Russians, the Soviet Union, came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: For the communists, it was a big day of celebration. For most of the other Jewish people in town, it was a very uncertain time. We didn't know what the new regime, so to speak, or what the new government, was going to be. What are they going to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: But in an indirect way, the Jewish people felt that it can't be any worse. It might be better. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] However, that also proved to be wrong. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The new government came in-- introduced their--

speaker: interviewer
text: Meaning the Russian government.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russian government introduced their style of governing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they had their own designs for the area. They weren't there to liberate anybody. They were there to pursue their goals. And now that we know how the Soviet Union acted, and how they progressed with their ideology, they first-- they first tried to declare who was their enemy. And they eliminated their enemy. In their philosophy and in their way of thinking, any merchant, any little storekeeper, any little free enterprise, or any person that made a living out for themself, for them, it was a-- a counterrevolutionary. It was a capitalist. It didn't fit their ideology.

speaker: interviewer
text: So first thing we find out is on a given day, their forces came in. Their police forces came in. And, within a few days, we found out that hundreds and hundreds of families have been forced, in the middle of the night, into trucks and disappeared. Were these Jewish families-- non-Jewish families?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly Jewish families, because Vishnitz was a Yiddish shtetl. There may have been some non-Jewish families, as well. But where we lived it was mostly Jewish people. The people that were gone were some of our friends, some of our neighbors, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] local storekeepers, local butcher shops, local people that were professional, but didn't quite fit the communist mold. Pardon me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where they were taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: At first, we didn't know. But we soon learned that most of them were sent off to Siberia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you learn that from?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it's-- it's kind of the type of news that communicates out of-- from word to mouth. And-- and people do find out. I know, because one of those people was my Uncle Moyche, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who was declared an enemy, simply because he lived in a large house and had a few trees of apples and-- and prunes and-- and things that grew in his own orchard. And he also, because he had a big house, and we lived on the outskirts of town, he had allowed to have a little synagogue in his house, so we could have-- so the Jewish people in that area could have prayers, Shabbos and Yontif. And also, because a local official thought that maybe he'd like to have his house.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one nice day, they asked them to come to the office, to this-- this country civil office. And they invited him. Of course, he had no idea what it was for. And we found out-- my family, my grandparents, and my dad-- my family found out the next day that he was taken to Chernovitz, by truck.

speaker: interviewee
text: They followed him into Chernovitz to find out where he was. They did see him, I believe, once. After that, he disappeared. And we never heard from him again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he taken with his family?

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- he was-- he had-- his wife was a very sick person. And she passed on. And he didn't have any children. So he was just by himself. That was my Uncle Moyche. That was the first casualty [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that was torn away from our family.

speaker: interviewer
text: About what year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That would have been in 1940, in 1940. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So while we lived under this communist system, there were many paradoxes. The communist ideology and systems are very hard to explain. They do things in a way which, to the average person that's brought up in a democratic and free society, is hard to understand. And I'll give you an example, for instance. The same Soviets, or the same government that took away people to Siberia and deprived people of their rights of ownership, the same people opened up a Yiddish school-- a Yiddish, I stress, Yiddish school-- that I remember, because I attended that school, had 630-some Yiddish students. All subjects were taught in Yiddish. Russian was a language by itself. And we were taught, one hour, Russian language. The teachers were all Yiddish, some local. I remember, because there's a Josif Burg who still lives in Chernovitz and does extensive writing in Yiddish. He was my Yiddish teacher. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And some were Jewish people, Yiddish people, from the Ukraine and from Russia. The director, I remember, was a name-- a gentleman by the name of Zabolotni, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who was a very nice man. I'll never forget him. What I'm trying to say is that on the one hand when I came home, life was depressed. And we feared the system. And when I went to school, I was elated by the fact that I could learn and learn in my own language. That was the paradox. And so life went on until one day. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I remember that day. It was on a Sunday. It was June the 22, 1941. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had amongst the kids a soccer game. And we were involved in that-- in our game. And we saw planes overhead. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we saw trucks running fast with sirens and with flags waving. And we soon found out that Germany had declared war on the Soviet Union. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Of course, all Jewish, able-bodied men were quickly taken to the Russian army. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And our situation was very uncertain. We feared that soon we will be overrun by the Germans and by the Romanians again. A lot of people rescued themselves or tried to rescue themselves by fleeing with the Russian authorities into the Soviet Union before the Germans came in. The local Ukrainian fascists started to smell blood. And they were, of course, out in full force to take advantage of whatever served them. And so a new era of uncertainty, war, started.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Thank you very much. We'll continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
